original_hypothesis.title: USD Price Governance Vote Exclusivity Violation Enables Double-Counting and Stuck Proposals
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The disapproveUSDPrice function checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` to prevent approvers from disapproving, but approveUSDPrice does NOT check `disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId`. A price feed operator can disapprove first, then subsequently approve the same proposal, incrementing both counters. This breaks vote mutual exclusivity and can cause proposals to become permanently stuck.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This weakens the multisig governance model for price updates. Under specific vote orderings, both approval and disapproval thresholds can be met simultaneously, causing _execUSDPriceUpdate to silently fail and the proposal to become stuck — no execution AND no deletion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approveUSDPrice checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` but never checks `disapprovals[msg.sender]`; disapproveUSDPrice sets disapprovals[msg.sender] but doesn't block later approval

preconditions: At least one price feed operator can call both disapprove and approve
Vote ordering allows disapprove before approve for at least one operator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: disapprovalsCount and approvalsCount can both be incremented by the same operator. In _execUSDPriceUpdate: `approvalsCount >= APPROVE_THRESHOLD && disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD` — if both thresholds met, update silently fails.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stuck proposal scenario: approval threshold reached but disapprovals also at threshold → _execUSDPriceUpdate does nothing, proposal stays (proposer != address(0)), blocking new proposals indefinitely until additional disapprovals force deletion.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Governance DoS — prevents price updates, causing stale prices that distort getLockedWeightedValue and downstream reward calculations.

economic_questions: What specific vote orderings lead to stuck proposals?
Can the stuck state be resolved without admin intervention?
What is the economic impact of stale USD prices on reward distribution?
validation_requirements: Identify stuck proposal conditions for default thresholds (3/3)
Quantify reward distortion from stale prices

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (only gas costs for price feed operators)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH gas per vote on Blast L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect — stale prices benefit some lockers over others depending on price direction
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable — depends on price staleness duration and price movement

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During volatile price movements when stale prices create maximum distortion
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Indirect — affects all users' weighted value calculations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users affected by stale token prices
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to token price movement during stuck period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model all vote orderings to find stuck states

validation_result.mechanism: missing cross-check in approveUSDPrice allows disapprover to also approve
validation_result.impact_summary: The approveUSDPrice function lacks a check for disapprovals[msg.sender], breaking vote mutual exclusivity. A price feed operator who disapproved can subsequently approve the same proposal, inflating both vote counters simultaneously. While the exact 'stuck proposal' outcome is unlikely with default thresholds due to immediate deletion on disapproval threshold, the code defect weakens the multisig governance model.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies a missing guard in approveUSDPrice. The disapproveUSDPrice function (lines ~218-227) checks BOTH approvals[msg.sender] and disapprovals[msg.sender], preventing an approver from also disapproving. However, approveUSDPrice (lines ~185-192) only checks approvals[msg.sender], never checking disapprovals[msg.sender]. This asymmetry allows an operator to: (1) call disapproveUSDPrice, setting disapprovals[msg.sender] = _usdProposalId and incrementing disapprovalsCount, then (2) call approveUSDPrice, which passes since approvals[msg.sender] is still 0 (not equal to _usdProposalId), setting approvals[msg.sender] = _usdProposalId and incrementing approvalsCount. Both counters are now inflated by the same voter.

Regarding the 'stuck proposal' claim: this is unlikely with default thresholds (3/3) because disapproveUSDPrice immediately deletes the proposal when disapprovalsCount >= DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD. Therefore, disapprovalsCount can never exceed (DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD - 1) while the proposal remains active. When _execUSDPriceUpdate is called, the maximum possible disapprovalsCount is (DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD - 1), which is always < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD, so the execution condition `disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD` would always pass. The stuck scenario requires both thresholds met simultaneously, but the disapproval threshold triggers immediate deletion before this state can be reached.

Nevertheless, the code defect is real: the mutual exclusivity invariant of votes is violated. This reduces the effective security of the multisig and can inflate vote counts beyond the number of unique operators.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 185-198 of approveUSDPrice contain four checks but none verify disapprovals[msg.sender]. Lines 218-227 of disapproveUSDPrice contain a proper cross-check (approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId). The asymmetry is clearly visible in the source code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing check: `if (usdUpdateProposal.disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId) revert ProposalAlreadyDisapprovedError();` — An operator who already disapproved can bypass all guards and also approve, incrementing both approvalsCount and disapprovalsCount for the same proposal.

This function correctly checks BOTH approvals[msg.sender] and disapprovals[msg.sender], preventing double-voting in either direction. The asymmetry with approveUSDPrice (which only checks approvals) is the root cause of the defect.

calling_sequence: 1. Operator B calls disapproveUSDPrice(_price): passes all checks since approvals[B] == 0 != _usdProposalId. Sets disapprovals[B] = _usdProposalId, disapprovalsCount++.

2. Operator B calls approveUSDPrice(_price): checks proposer != address(0) ✓, proposer != B ✓, approvals[B] (== 0) != _usdProposalId ✓, price matches ✓. All checks pass! Sets approvals[B] = _usdProposalId, approvalsCount++.
3. Both counters are now inflated by operator B's double vote.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in approveUSDPrice at lines 191-192. The function checks `usdUpdateProposal.approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` (line 191) to prevent double-approval, but never checks `usdUpdateProposal.disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId`. In contrast, disapproveUSDPrice at line 220 checks `usdUpdateProposal.approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` to prevent an approver from disapproving. This one-directional guard creates the asymmetric vulnerability.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts set APPROVE_THRESHOLD or DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD. Defaults of 3/3 are hardcoded in contract state declarations at lines 17-20.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
The disapprove-then-approve path is confirmed as valid. However, the 'stuck proposal' outcome described in the hypothesis is NOT achievable with the current code because disapproveUSDPrice immediately deletes the proposal when disapprovalsCount >= DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD (line 237-239). This means disapprovalsCount can never reach DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD while the proposal remains alive for _execUSDPriceUpdate to evaluate. The maximum disapprovalsCount when _execUSDPriceUpdate is called is (DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD - 1), which always satisfies `disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD`. Therefore the proposal will execute successfully if approvals reach the threshold.

The real impact is governance weakening: the multisig's vote integrity is compromised since individual operators can vote in both directions, inflating total vote counts beyond the number of unique operators.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The code defect breaks vote mutual exclusivity (INV-ACCESS-002), which is a clear logic error. The most severe claimed impact (stuck proposals) is not achievable due to immediate deletion on disapproval threshold, but the defect still undermines governance integrity: (1) Vote counts can exceed the number of unique operators, (2) Disapproval votes become partially meaningless since operators can switch to approval afterwards, (3) The semantic meaning of disapproval is weakened as it doesn't preclude later approval.
recommended_mitigations: Add a check in approveUSDPrice: `if (usdUpdateProposal.disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId) revert ProposalAlreadyDisapprovedError();`
Consider a unified voting mechanism with an enum (NONE, APPROVE, DISAPPROVE) per operator per proposal to enforce mutual exclusivity at the storage level
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with 5 price feed operators and default thresholds (3/3). 2. Operator A calls proposeUSDPrice(1000, [tokenAddr]). 3. Operator B calls disapproveUSDPrice(1000) — succeeds, disapprovalsCount=1. 4. Operator B calls approveUSDPrice(1000) — should revert but succeeds, approvalsCount=2, disapprovalsCount=1. 5. Verify both approvals[B] and disapprovals[B] equal _usdProposalId. 6. Operator C calls approveUSDPrice(1000) — approvalsCount=3, triggers execution. 7. Verify proposal executes successfully (because disapprovalsCount=1 < 3). 8. Verify the code defect: operator B effectively cast two votes.
uncertainties: The stuck proposal scenario claimed in the hypothesis appears impossible with any threshold configuration due to immediate deletion on disapproval threshold, but edge cases with admin threshold changes between proposals may warrant further testing.
The practical governance impact depends on whether operators are independent entities or potentially colluding.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test all possible vote orderings with 5 operators to confirm no stuck state is reachable
Verify behavior if admin sets extreme thresholds (e.g., APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1, DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=5)
Test if the double-vote effectively reduces the security model from 3-of-5 to 2-of-5 in terms of operator collusion
